coredata.dc:title: Crystalline F-doped titanium dioxide nanoparticles decorated with graphene quantum dots for improving the photodegradation of water pollutants
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Gómez, I. Jénnifer
Díaz-Sánchez, Miguel
Pizúrová, Naděžda
Zajíčková, Lenka
Prashar, Sanjiv
Gómez-Ruiz, Santiago
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots are emerging photoactive materials with high chemical stability, aqueous solubility, abundant surface functional groups and low-cost production. Their great advantages, incorporated into the high photocatalytic activity of the TiO2, result in hybrid systems that overcome some of the photocatalytic drawbacks associated with TiO2. In this work, a facile synthesis of hybrids of F-doped TiO2 and N-doped graphene quantum dots (F-TiO2@N-GQDs) is reported. These systems have demonstrated efficient photocatalytic properties in light-driven pollutant reduction from water. Therefore, using a simple and low-cost synthesis method, the N-GQDs act as electron reservoirs improving the pairs e--h+ lifetime in TiO2 by decreasing charge recombination, increasing their photocatalytic capacity. The photocatalysts showed very effective degradations of different contaminants such as methylene blue (90% degradation) ciprofloxacin (62% degradation) and naproxen (60% degradation) in short periods of up to 15 min and 4-chlorophenol (59% degradation) in 30 min using UV light (300 W).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Microwave
Titanium dioxide
Functionalization
Photocatalysis
Wastewater treatment

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Operational Program Research, Development and Education-Project “MSCAfellow4@MUNI” (CZ.02.2.69/0.0/0.0/20_079/0017045) and the Spanish Ministry of Universities for a Maria Zambrano funding (UP2021-001) transferred by the European Union-Next GenerationEU. The authors acknowledge CzechNanoLab Research Infrastructure (LM2018110) supported by the Ministry of Education, Youth and Sports of the Czech Republic (MEYS CR). The authors are grateful to Prof. Petr Klán, and Prof. Vladimír Šindelář for allowing to use of the UV–Vis and fluorescence spectrometer, and the MW reactor. Financial resources related to this equipment were RECETOX research infrastructure (supported by MEYS CR under LM2018121 and 02.1.01/0.0/0.0/18_046/0015975) and the CETOCOEN EXCELLENCE Teaming 2 project Horizon2020 (857560). We would also like to thank the project TED2021-132175B-I00 funded by MCIN/AEI/10.13039/501100011033 and by the “European Union NextGenerationEU / PRTR”.

This work was supported by Operational Program Research, Development and Education-Project “MSCAfellow4@MUNI” (CZ.02.2.69/0.0/0.0/20_079/0017045) and the Spanish Ministry of Universities for a Maria Zambrano funding (UP2021-001) transferred by the European Union-Next GenerationEU. The authors acknowledge CzechNanoLab Research Infrastructure (LM2018110) supported by the Ministry of Education, Youth and Sports of the Czech Republic (MEYS CR). The authors are grateful to Prof. Petr Klán, and Prof. Vladimír Šindelář for allowing to use of the UV–Vis and fluorescence spectrometer, and the MW reactor. Financial resources related to this equipment were RECETOX research infrastructure (supported by MEYS CR under LM2018121 and 02.1.01/0.0/0.0/18_046/0015975) and the CETOCOEN EXCELLENCE Teaming 2 project Horizon2020 (857560). We would also like to thank the project TED2021-132175B-I00 funded by MCIN/AEI/10.13039/501100011033 and by the “European Union NextGenerationEU / PRTR”.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Representative scheme for the formation of corresponding (a, b) pristine materials and the (c) hybrid formation of F-TiO2@N-GQDs photocatalyst.

Chemical structure and composition of F-TiO2@N-GQDs photocatalyst and their starting materials N-GQDs and F-TiO2: (a) FTIR spectra. (b) XRD patterns. Lower concentrations were not displayed in the graphs (i.e., 0.5% and 0.25 %) since no differences were expected or observed. (c) Photographs of the F-TiO2@N-GQDs photocatalyst. (d) XPS survey spectra. High resolution peaks of (e) C 1 s, (b) N 1 s and (c) O 1 s.

Morphology of F-TiO2@N-GQDs (5%) photocatalyst and F-TiO2. (a) SEM image of pristine F-TiO2. (b) SEM image of F-TiO2@N-GQDs (5%). (c) EDS image of F-TiO2@N-GQDs (5%) overlayed with the composite elemental map; (d) EDS mapping of F, (e) EDS mapping of O, (f) EDS mapping of Ti, (g) EDS mapping of N and (h) EDS mapping of C. The scale bars represent 10 μm.

Optical properties of the F-TiO2@N-GQDs photocatalyst. (a, b, c) Diffuse reflectance ultraviolet spectroscopy (DR-UV) spectrum. (d, e, f) Kubelka–Munk function versus energy (eV) for bandgap calculation (i.e., the optimal range for the band gap calculation is represented with the red line). (g, h, i) Solid state fluorescence emission at different excitation wavelengths.
(a) Photocatalytic activity of MB using F-TiO2@N-GQDs under UV light (36 W). (b) Recyclability of the photocatalytic degradation of MB using F-TiO2@N-GQDs (1%) under UV-light (300 W).
Proposed mechanism for the photocatalytic degradation F-TiO2@N-GQDs under UV–vis light irradiation.
Study of the photocatalytic degradation of 20 mL of aqueous solutions of different pollutant using 10 mg of F-TiO2@N-GQDs (1%) as photocatalyst under UV light and visible light (300 W).

head.ce:title: Crystalline F-doped titanium dioxide nanoparticles decorated with graphene quantum dots for improving the photodegradation of water pollutants

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots are emerging photoactive materials with high chemical stability, aqueous solubility, abundant surface functional groups and low-cost production. Their great advantages, incorporated into the high photocatalytic activity of the TiO2, result in hybrid systems that overcome some of the photocatalytic drawbacks associated with TiO2. In this work, a facile synthesis of hybrids of F-doped TiO2 and N-doped graphene quantum dots (F-TiO2@N-GQDs) is reported. These systems have demonstrated efficient photocatalytic properties in light-driven pollutant reduction from water. Therefore, using a simple and low-cost synthesis method, the N-GQDs act as electron reservoirs improving the pairs e--h+ lifetime in TiO2 by decreasing charge recombination, increasing their photocatalytic capacity. The photocatalysts showed very effective degradations of different contaminants such as methylene blue (90% degradation) ciprofloxacin (62% degradation) and naproxen (60% degradation) in short periods of up to 15 min and 4-chlorophenol (59% degradation) in 30 min using UV light (300 W).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the fast industrial development and growth that is being experienced nowadays, environmental pollution represents a vast area of a worldwide pressing challenge associated with several SDGs of the 2030 Agenda, especially with SDG 6 Clean Water and Sanitation.

In this framework, global efforts must pave the way to mitigate environmental pollution and protect human health, providing new and/or enhanced solutions to the urgent environmental problems (i.e., from water pollution, soil to air pollution). For instance, the improvement of photocatalysis research, specifically used in advanced oxidation processes for wastewater treatment (AOPs) [1], is imperative and is situated at the forefront of the technological worldwide problems of the third millennium. Among the numerous photocatalysts that have been developed, such as ZnO [2,3], SnO2 [4], CeO2 [5], TiO2 is one of the most promising because of its physical and chemical stability, low toxicity and other interesting properties [6]. Despite the continuous signs of progress, some concerns on TiO2 photocatalysts need to be undertaken to their final translation to practical applications on an industrial level. In detail, TiO2 present a low efficient use of visible light caused by wide band gap (3.0 ∼ 3.2 eV) as well as their high recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs that limit their potential applications on a large scale [7,8]. Many of these problems can be overcome by several strategies, such as doping [9–12], tailoring their structure with semiconductors [13], or introducing carbon materials [14–16].

Following the above premises, the development of carbon-based materials is rising up on environmental applications such as adsorptive removal of contaminants [17,18], catalytic degradation and transformations, and energy conversion [19,20]. In addition, it has been proven that the use of the carbon species as supports or additives on TiO2 give hybrid materials with improved activity [21,22]. Particularly, carbon dots (CDs) [23–25] the last to join the widespread carbon family have attracted intensive attention in the field of photocatalysis [26,27], due to the large heterogeneity in functional groups apart from a set of useful properties (i.e., excellent chemical and photochemical stability, good biocompatibility, high-water solubility and low toxicity), which could be beneficial to promote a fast electron transfer on the structure interfaces of the photocatalyst [26]. Although typical CDs present optical absorption in the UV–Vis region [23,28], which could limit their translation to practical applications, it is well known that, tuning the surface of the dots structure with atom doping, could enhance their optical properties [29]. For instance, nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) have shown better performance than unmodified CDs on electron delocalization and charge carrier density [30].

In this context, the combination of TiO2 and CDs could be the gold solution. The preparation of hybrid systems based on carbon dots-TiO2 is relatively simple from the synthesis point of view and can afford complex structures with various functional groups. In detail, CDs (i.e., carbon quantum dots) combined with TiO2 have been reported for their use as efficient photocatalytic hydrogen evolution applications [31]. Therefore, their translation in wastewater remediation could be prominent applying the most recent advances in the photocatalytic area.

Herein, a straightforward protocol to prepare an innovative photocatalyst based on the well-known fluorine-doped TiO2 (F-TiO2) nanoparticles [32], and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) is reported. The use of F-doped titanium oxide instead of the non-doped counterpart is because of the improvement that this dopant is able to produce on the photocatalyst. F-doping results in a slight decrease of the band gap, a reduction in the recombination rate e--h+ and the generation of charge capture centers caused by the formation of Ti3+ ions and oxygen vacancies. In addition, F-doping of titanium dioxide provokes a greater presence of ultra-reactive {001} facets, which improves the surface characteristics and electronic properties of the oxide with respect to {101} facets (majority in non-doped titanium oxides), as well as a slight increase in surface area that results in increased photocatalyst-substrate interaction. It is also important to note that F-doping can generate the formation of surface Ti-F groups that act as capture sites for photogenerated electrons, facilitating their transfer to adsorbed O2 molecules with interesting improvements in photodegradation processes. This type of oxide has been widely used as a photocatalytic material in several studies carried out by our research group, where all the benefits described above have been demonstrated [9,33,34]. However, TiO2 suffers from a high rate of electronic recombination. In this sense, the novelty of the present study is supported on the incorporation of carbon species that act as electron reservoir, decreasing this recombination and increasing the photocatalytic capacity of already highly reactive photocatalyst. The advantage of N-GQDs over other carbon-based systems of this type is the use of materials of low toxicity for the organism [25,35], which do not limit the use of these photosystems in a future application for an effective and a harmless decontamination of water for human consumption.

The final novel hybrid was optimized and characterized in detail, showing excellent textural and optical properties. In addition, this work shows the high potential of the studied hybrid systems for the photocatalytic treatment of water pollutants, whose mechanism has been studied in detail to explain the material roles using different scavenger methods.

The reported properties of the studied systems of this work open up the possibility of a more thorough investigation of analogous systems for their future application in other photochemical or photocatalytic processes in environmental chemistry.

The photocatalyst based on F-TiO2@N-GQDs was prepared as follows: to a solution of F-TiO2 (250 mg) in MeOH (30 mL) a certain amount of N-GQDs was added. Different concentrations of N-GQDs in percent weight were added (5%, 2%, 1%, 0.5% and 0.25%). The mixture was sonicated for 1 h and then the resulting mixture was stirred at room temperature for 16 h. The solid products were isolated and washed several times using ultrapure water and ethanol. The resulting materials were named F-TiO2@N-GQDs (5%), F-TiO2@N-GQDs (2%), F-TiO2@N-GQDs (1%), F-TiO2@N-GQDs (0.5%), F-TiO2@N-GQDs (0.25%). For comparative purposes, the commercial titanium oxide nanopowder Aeroxide® P25 was also used in the photocatalytic study of the synthesized materials (TiO2-P25).

The FTIR spectra were acquired with an Alpha FTIR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). XRD pattern was measured on a Phillips diffractometer PW3040/00 X’Pert MPD/MRD. Thermogravimmetry analyses (TGA) were carried out with a Shimadzu mod. DSC-50Q operating between 30 and 800 °C (ramp 20 °C/min) at an intensity of 50 A in nitrogen. The XPS was performed with an Axis Supra spectrometer (Kratos Analytical Ltd, UK). SEM-EDS were recorded with a Verios 460L instrument (FEI, USA). AFM images were taken with a Dimension Icon (Bruker, Germany). N2 gas adsorption–desorption isotherms (BET) were performed using a Micromeritics ASAP 2020 porosimeter. The absorption spectra were recorded on a Varian Cary 500 UV–Vis (Agilent Technologies, USA). Fluorescence spectra were measured on a F900 Fluorescent spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd, UK). Additional details are given in Supplementary Information.

F-doped titanium dioxide mesoporous aggregates of nanoparticles (F-TiO2) were synthesized as previously reported [32], based on a simple protocol, which consists of the hydrolysis and condensation of titanium(IV) isopropoxide, Ti(OiPr)4 (TTIP), in a solution of nitric acid in ultrapure water at pH 2 using NaF as a doping agent followed by calcination at 500 °C. The resulting nanoparticles displayed sizes around 12.90 ± 2.19 nm (Fig. S1) and anatase phase (Fig. 2b), as revealed by X-ray diffraction [32].

In addition, the N-GQDs were prepared from glucose and ethylenediamine following our previously reported low-cost and effective approach using microwave hydrothermal synthesis [35]. The nanoparticles presented quasispherical shapes with an average size of 5.7 ± 0.1 nm (Fig. S2), and various oxygen and nitrogen functional groups on the surface. They exhibited excitation wavelength-dependent emission behavior from the near-ultraviolet up to the near-infrared range. The previously published results suggested that the N-doping might assist in the homogeneous distribution of metal nanoparticles on the surface of the dots, which have shown influence in the electron delocalization and charge carrier density [30,36,37]. These properties motivated us to prepare a hybrid system based on F-TiO2@N-GQDs that will offer more versatility in light-driven photocatalysis because the hybrid, with enhanced absorption, might allow multiple electronic excitations under light irradiation.

As depicted in Fig. 1 , the preparation of the hybrid, F-TiO2@N-GQDs, was based on the direct assembly of the N-GQDs onto the F-TiO2 surface by surface interactions. An optimization of the photocatalyst was carried out by preparing various concentration ratios of material (i.e., 5%, 2%, 1%, 0.5% and 0.25% based on N-GQDs loading), to determine if some effect on the photocatalytic activity is possible. Afterward, the hybrids were purified by centrifugation to remove the excess of N-GQDs that were not attached onto the F-TiO2 surface. The resulting materials were named F-TiO2@N-GQDs (5%), F-TiO2@N-GQDs (2%), F-TiO2@N-GQDs (1%), F-TiO2@N-GQDs (0.5%), F-TiO2@N-GQDs (0.25%).

All FTIR spectra of the F-TiO2@N-GQDs photocatalysts showed the characteristic infrared signals of titanium dioxide, such as the Ti-O-Ti stretching band between ca. 900 and 450 cm−1 (Fig. 2 a) [32]. Due to the low amount of N-GQDs loaded and the high intensity of F-TiO2 signals, almost no characteristic signals from the dots were recognized [35]. However, for 5 % N-GQDs, a slightly increased absorption compared with the pristine F-TiO2 in the range 3500–3000 cm−1 was observed and attributed to the presence of N-H and O-H stretching vibrations of the dots, confirming their incorporation to the system. The stretching vibration observed at around ∼2859 and ∼1638 cm−1 were associated with C-H, C=C, C=N, N-H and C=O, while peaks at ∼1276 and ∼1085 cm−1, corresponded to C-O-C groups, respectively.

Similarly, the XRD patterns of the F-TiO2@N-GQDs were in good agreement with the XRD data of F-TiO2 (Fig. 2b and Fig. S3) [32]. No characteristic peaks of N-GQDs were detected, which may be because of their relatively low diffraction intensity compared to that of F-TiO2. It is important to note that, in all cases, the diffractograms show that the main component of the samples is anatase phase (with almost no existence of either brookite or rutile crystalline phases). It is important to note that, in general, anatase presents the most attractive photocatalytic behavior due to its electronical and textural properties [38]. Anatase phase is maintained in all systems regardless of the percentage of N-GQDs functionalization. Therefore, it can be concluded that the surface modification with dots species does not influence the crystalline phases of F-TiO2 once it has been formed. Apart from that, one can easily observe that the color changes from white to brownish when the concentration of N-GQDs increases (Fig. 2c).

Additionally, XPS studies were carried for the hybrid system with the highest percentage of N-GQDs (i.e., F-TiO2@N-GQDs (5%)) to observe some characteristics signals from the dots. Pristine N-GQDs contained mainly elemental carbon, nitrogen and oxygen (Fig. 2d, Fig. S4-6 and Table S1) [35], whereas F-TiO2@N-GQDs also displayed the presence of titanium (22.52 at. %) and fluorine (0.53 at. %) apart from carbon (25.53 at. %), nitrogen (2.10 at. %) and oxygen (48.85 at. %), as shown in Fig. 2d (Fig. S7,S8 and Table S2). It is noteworthy that the pristine F-TiO2 did not present N or C atoms, however the presence of the latter is due to adventitious carbon (284.8 eV, Fig. S9-S11 and Table S3). Thus, the modification of the dots with F-TiO2 nanoparticles was confirmed. It is important to note that no significant differences were observed in the high-resolution carbon and nitrogen spectrum of F-TiO2@N-GQDs from the dots (Fig. 2e and Fig. 2f) [35], however, the oxygen high-resolution spectrum displayed a new component corresponding to the Ti-O (Fig. 2g and Table S2), which further verifies the hybrid formation.

In the prepared F-TiO2@N-GQDs hybrid, the degree of organic loading of the system was determined by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), as was already analyzed in other structures[39–41]. Overall, the theoretical mass ratio of N-GQDs in the most effective TiO2 is 1%, a TGA of this material with respect to the pristine F-TiO2 system was carried out to determine the functionalization degree (Fig. S12). The results obtained from the mass loss of material F-TiO2@N-GQDs (1%) compared with those of pristine F-TiO2 material showed a presence of an organic load of 0.75 %, which indicate that the functionalization of the system with N-GQDs is fitting with the proposed theoretical value, which together with all the other characterization studies confirm the feasibility of the synthetic method used in the study.

The size and morphology of the as-prepared F-TiO2@N-GQDs systems were analyzed by atomic force and scanning electron microscopies (AFM, SEM), and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). AFM and SEM micrographs revealed that F-TiO2 and F-TiO2@N-GQDs presented obvious aggregation (Fig. S13,S14 and Fig. 3 ab). N-GQDs are nanoparticles with an average size of around 4 nm [35], whereas F-TiO2 of around 12.90 ± 2.19 nm [32,33]. After conjugation of the pristine materials, a fairly increased size was observed in comparison with the nano size of carbon dots. Albeit due to the aggregation of the final hybrids the specific size could not be studied, further noticeable morphological differences were observed between F-TiO2 and F-TiO2@N-GQDs in higher magnification views (Fig. 3ab and Fig. S13,S14). After the dots loading onto the F-TiO2 surface, agglomerations of spherical granules were observed. Since F-TiO2 nanoparticles have a crystalline symmetry with exposed facets of different morphological shape, the generation of a spherical morphology indicates that dots occupied a certain volume onto the F-TiO2 surface. Furthermore, energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) was carried out to evaluate the elemental composition. The EDS spectrum and mapping of F-TiO2@N-GQDs hybrids indicated the elemental distribution of fluorine, oxygen, titanium, nitrogen and carbon over the particles, confirming that the dots were well dispersed onto the F-TiO2 (Fig. 3c-d and Fig. S15). On the other hand, the EDS spectrum and mapping of F-TiO2 displayed three peaks (Fig. S16,S17): fluorine, oxygen and titanium indicating that N-GQDs successfully were doped into F-TiO2. All the aforementioned results were in agreement with the findings revealed by XPS.

The most promising photocatalytic system F-TiO2@N-GQDs (1%) showed a typical type III isotherm according to the IUPAC classification [42], (Fig. S18) with values of N2 gas adsorption–desorption isotherms (BET) surface area (SBET), BHJ average pore diameter (Dp), and pore volume (Vp) of 44.17 m2/g, 0.27 cm3/g and 24.56 Å respectively. The values are in the mesoporous range (2–50 nm) and its surface area is similar or superior to those found for similar titanium dioxide nanostructured systems [33]. This indicates that the GQDs do not cause surface saturation of the material at the studied percentage, maintaining a high photocatalyst-pollutant interaction.

The optical properties of the as-prepared F-TiO2@N-GQDs materials were also assessed. The optical absorption of the F-TiO2 (diffuse reflectance, Fig. 4 a-c and Fig. S19,S21) is modified after the functionalization with the NGQDs. The changes in the optical absorption are attributed to the carbon bonding and oxygen vacancies formed in the F-TiO2 surface[34,43]. Nevertheless, when analyzing a 1% carbon content increment, almost no modifications were observed in the optical absorption since it will not increase the chemical defects or vacancies present in the intergranular regions and the chemical interaction between NGQDs and F-TiO2.

Unlike graphene, GQDs exhibited a non-zero band gap. The optical band gap was calculated from the Tauc plot in the UV–Vis-NIR spectrum (Fig. S19) [44]. For a direct band gap material, the band gap energy (Eg) of N-GQDs was evaluated as 4.45 eV. This large band gap can be due to the strong negative induction effect of graphite N in GQDs [4546]. On the other hand, the band gap of the samples was calculated by using diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (Fig. 4a-f and Fig. S20,S21). Based on the difference in their band gap energies and their contact, an interface at the heterojunction between N-GQDs and F-TiO2 materials might be formed. This phenomenon may lead to charge transfer or migration across the heterojunction. In general, the band gap values increased when the concentration of dots was risen (Table S4), which is in agreement with other F-TiO2 systems with surface functionalization [33]. Besides, a band gap stabilization at 1% of carbon content was observed for F-TiO2@N-GQDs, which is related to the saturation point achievement. In all cases, the band gap energies are between 3.03 and 3.35 eV, corresponding to typical values for the anatase phase of F-TiO2 (the major component of the photocatalytic hybrid obtained) and do not appear to be responsible for the significant differences found in the photocatalytic degradation experiments which are more closely associated with the electronic characteristics of N-GQDs. This phenomenon is probably related to the enhancement of the electron–hole separation [47]. The fluorescence emission in solid-state of F-TiO2@N-GQDs showed the excitation-dependent emission behavior typical of the N-GQDs that may arise from different radiative recombination pathways (Fig. 4g-i and Fig. S22) [35]. Thus, the modification of the dots with F-TiO2 did not affect their unique emission properties. Nevertheless, a red-shift in the optimal excitation and emission wavelength (λex and λem) from λex 380 nm λem 466 nm in the dots [35], to λex 480 nm λem 580 nm F-TiO2@N-GQDs (5%) for the hybrids was observed (Fig. 4g). Furthermore, the λex and λem of the hybrid blue-shift as the concentration of dots was decreasing. In detail, from λex 480 nm λem 580 nm for F-TiO2@N-GQDs (5%) to λex 440 nm λem 540 nm F-TiO2@N-GQDs (0.25%) (Fig. S22). Overall, a decrease in the fluorescence intensity was observed (Fig. 4g-i and Fig. S22). The last modification is probably due to the combination of effects such as aggregation in solid-state and energy transfer from the F-TiO2 nanoparticles [47].

A comparative study of the different hybrids obtained was carried out in order to determine which of them showed the most promising photocatalytic behavior. The kinetic study displayed in Fig. 5 a was determined by measuring the MB absorbance at different time intervals, which allowed the calculation of the photocatalytic degradation kinetic constant (Table S5). In detail, both the amount of MB adsorbed (i.e., 1 h in the dark) and the amounts of MB degraded were measured at 30 min and 1 h (Table S5). Clearly, Fig. 5a suggested an increase in the photodegradation of MB in comparison with the pristine F-TiO2 at an optimal N-GQDs 1% doping F-TiO2@N-GQDs (1%), (Fig. 5a yellow trace and Table S5). Furthermore, the kinetic constant was higher for F-TiO2@N-GQDs (1%) 6.7·10-4 s−1 than pristine F-TiO2 5.3·10-4 s−1, which could be related to the adsorption of MB molecules and the active points of the surface material. On the other hand, increasing the doping of N-GQDs could saturate or inactivate the active points of the material. The comparative of photocatalysts with different proportions of N-GQDs loaded onto the F-TiO2 in the study of MB elimination above did not show a clear relationship between the initial adsorption of pollutants and their subsequent removal. As verified in previous studies performed by our research group [34], unmodified F-TiO2 shows again a superior photodegradation activity compared with that of commercial TiO2-P25 oxide (Table S5 and Fig. S23), supporting the morphological and electronic advantages that F-doping exerts on the system. This photocatalytic enhancement becomes more evident together with the optimal doping of N-GQDs 1% F-TiO2@N-GQDs (1%) making the hybrid material an optimal system for this type of processes, (Fig. 5a yellow trace and Table S5), with better performance than commercial titanium oxide (Table S5 and Fig. S23).

Additionally, the most promising system in terms of photocatalysis, namely, F-TiO2@N-GQDs (1%) was tested on emergent organic contaminants in an aqueous medium, such as anti-inflammatory drugs (naproxen, NPX), antibiotics (ciprofloxacin, CPX) and industrial pollutants (4-chlorophenol, 4-CP and methylene blue, MB) was carried out (Table 1 ). Overall, the results showed degradation values around 60 % in all cases at 30 min. These results indicate the good potential of the studied material F-TiO2@N-GQDs (1%) in photocatalytic degradation of emerging pollutants in water. In fact, the pollutants with the lowest initial adsorption percentage (i.e., CPX and NPX) showed a more pronounced elimination at lower UV light application time than in the case of 4-CP. Thus, this could be related to the electronic effects (more specifically those associated with a low electronic recombination) that benefits the photocatalytic process. Interestingly, the estimated performances for carbon-modified TiO2-based photocatalyst for the degradation of several pollutants were comparable with the best yields reported in the literature (see Table S6). In the case of the MB, the degradation was comparable if not somewhat better than other reported systems. It should be noted that using carbon-related dots as a carbon source, the best performance was observed in our system for just 60 min.

In order to determine the potential applicability of the F-TiO2@N-GQDs, the photocatalytic behavior under visible light of the optimized F-TiO2@N-GQDs (1%) was tested (Table 1). Interestingly, the photocatalytic material was active in the degradation of the studied emerging pollutants showing degradation values around 50% in all cases at 180 min. Nevertheless, the photocatalytic degradation of the pollutants under visible light was lower compared with UV light. All these results indicate the very high potential of the studied material in photocatalytic degradation of emerging pollutants in water, using a cost-efficient source such as solar light, for example.

Finally, a recyclability study was carried out for the MB photodegradation under UV light (Fig. 5b). The photocatalytic degradation of MB slightly decreases after each cycle. In detail, the photocatalytic material removed around 50 % of the MB in 10 min after the fifth cycle of use (Table S7). Besides, after the first cycle the material tend to increment the adsorption of MB molecules due to affinity with the surface of the material that already presented MB molecules. The diffractogram of F-TiO2@N-GQDs (1%) after cleaning with ethanol and dry to remove de pollutant upon the 5th photocatalytic cycle (see Fig. S24) was performed. The results verified that the material maintains its anatase structure, indicating that the system seems to be stable and does not suffer any modification in its crystallinity after the consecutive photocatalytic cycles of the recyclability process.

The effects of the reduction in photoactivity can be explained in terms of surface saturation. The blue color of the F-TiO2 nanoparticles after each process (that gets darker after each cycle) and the corresponding increase in the retention after each recyclability process (see Table S7) show that adsorption removal increases to the detriment of photodegradation. The saturation provoked by the dye prevents its effective contact with the surface of the photocatalyst and the correct interaction between the light and the material, which decreases the photoactivity after each cycle, although this is maintained at values of more than 50% after five consecutive cycles, with a relatively low amount of material and without being treated after each process.

To shed light on the possible photocatalytic mechanism of action in the degradation of the wastewater pollutants, the radicals generated during the photodegradation were studied following reported scavenger methods (i.e., charge quenching experiments), by determining which species are involved in the charge transfer process and how they interact with the photocatalyst [9,33,48]. Typically, TiO2 based-photocatalyst mechanism primarily involves the generation of hydroxyl radicals (•OH). Thus, in a first study the (•OH) formed in the photocatalytic reaction of F-TiO2@N-GQDs (1%) were analyzed and confirmed by adding NaCl (further details Supporting Information, Fig. S25), a decrease in the photocatalytic activity was observed. This decrease in the kinetics of the process and in the percentage of elimination was a consequence of the formation of ClOH− species, by reaction of Cl− with •OH radicals [49]. This indicates that the •OH radicals are essential in the photocatalytic process. The kinetic constant was reduced from 7.6 × 10-4 s−1 to 5.1 × 10-4 s−1, with a decrease in the percentage of photocatalytic degradation from 82 % to 63 % in 30 min (see the kinetic study in Fig. S22 and Table S8). In addition, there was a clear decrease in the adsorption percentage with the presence of NaCl. The salt in solution presumably generated the formation of a layer on the surface of the dispersed photocatalyst, inhibiting the proper approach of the MB molecules and slowing down the photocatalytic process [50].

On the other hand, the transformation of coumarin-3-carboxylic acid (3-CCA) with F-TiO2@N-GQDs (1%) was evaluated [48]. 3-CCA was proposed as a scavenger for the detection of •OH radicals formed under UV light from the F-TiO2@N-GQDs photocatalyst. Thus, the above discussed statement was reinforced, as the photoreaction liberated or/and formed •OH radicals, which led to a continuous decreased in the absorbance (Fig. S26).

In line with the obtained results, the proposed photocatalytic mechanism is given in Fig. 6 . Under UV–Vis light irradiation, the electrons situated in the valence band (VB) of TiO2 were excited to the conduction band (CB). Besides, N-GQDs, which might act as electron reservoir and solid-state electron transfer, trap the photoinduced electrons from the CB of F-TiO2, which is beneficial to the efficient separation of electrons and holes [51]. Thus, the holes left in the VB of TiO2 may react with water leading to its decomposition and forming •OH radicals homogeneously distributed in the solution via a photo-oxidation reaction. Furthermore, the electrons situated on the N-GQDs HOMO level could be excited by absorbing photons and then transfer them to the CB of TiO2 due to its deeper energy level. Then, one cannot rule out that the accumulated electrons in TiO2 could react with O2 to give a photo-reduction process to generate •O2 – [52]. Lastly, it seems that the studied hybrid photocatalyst fit with the Langmuir-Hinshelwood model with an adsorption constant KLH of the reaction of 29 mM−1, and an intrinsic reaction rate constant kL obtained of 2.5 × 10-5 mM/s (further details Supporting Information, Fig. S27) [9,33]. A photocatalytic reaction proceeds through this type of mechanism when a linear reciprocal relationship exists between the reaction rate and the observed concentration of substrate in a solution. This model indicates that the process starts after the adsorption–desorption equilibrium process on the external surface of the photocatalyst, which makes this stage one of the most important processes together with the reaction between •OH radicals and the adsorbed pollutant. The result agrees with those obtained when NaCl is applied, where the adsorption decreases with respect to the reaction without the presence of salt, negatively affecting the kinetics of the process. However, the low values of the constants obtained, especially KL, indicate that despite their importance, other factors govern the photocatalytic process of the F-TiO2@N-GQDs (1%) material, especially those associated with the decrease in electronic recombination due to the presence of N-GQDs, which is confirmed by the low emission profiles in the PL (Fig. 4g-i). This means that the generated charge carriers are able to move to the surface of the photocatalyst and participate in reactions before they recombine.

In conclusion, we have reported a hybrid system based on the combination of F-TiO2 and N-GQDs by using a ready, facile and effective adsorption conjugation protocol. Structural and compositional characterizations revealed the successful attachment of GQDs to titanium dioxide, resulting photocatalyst showed fluorescent properties and a red-shift in the optimal excitation and emission wavelength compared to pristine dots. The hybrid system provides enhanced photocatalytic activity as compared to the well know F-doped titanium dioxide and, thus, the hybrid system F-TiO2@N-GQDs has been proven to be an efficient photocatalyst for the degradation of light-driven pollutants reduction from water, such as methylene blue, 4-chlorophenol, ciprofloxacin and naproxen, even using visible light, also showing an acceptable degree of recyclability.

I. Jénnifer Gómez: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization. Miguel Díaz-Sánchez: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Naděžda Pizúrová: Data curation. Lenka Zajíčková: Writing – review & editing. Sanjiv Prashar: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Santiago Gómez-Ruiz: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
